content.problemStatement:
Chef is attempting to learn magic, but it really hasn't been going very well.

In order to pass an upcoming exam, Chef decides to just memorize magic spells - but even that turns out to be too hard!

A magic spell is, quite simply, a string that consists of only lowercase Latin letters (i.e, the characters {a,b,c…,z}\{\texttt{a,b,c}\ldots,\texttt{z}\}{a,b,c…,z}).

Chef has with him a magic spell SSS, and is struggling to remember it.

To make things easier, he decides to delete exactly one character from it - after all, a small modification to the spell won't change its effect, right?

Chef believes he has a better chance of remembering the spell if it's lexicographically smallest.

Can you help Chef find which character he should delete so that the resulting string is lexicographically smallest among all possible results?

**Note:** Lexicographic order is essentially dictionary order.

That is, string AAA is lexicographically smaller than string BBB if, at the first index iii where they differ, Ai<BiA_i \lt B_iAi​<Bi​.

For instance, ac\texttt{ac}ac is smaller than ad\texttt{ad}ad and ba\texttt{ba}ba, but not ab\texttt{ab}ab.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, the length of Chef's spell.
The second line contains the string SSS, representing the spell itself.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the lexicographically smallest string Chef can obtain by deleting a single character from SSS.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 300000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N (the length of the string), the second line contains a string S of length N consisting only of lowercase Latin letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the lexicographically smallest string that can be obtained by deleting exactly one character from S.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case we delete the first character that is larger than its next character. In "ba" we delete 'b' → "a". In "xxyy" the first decreasing pair is at the second 'x' (x > y), so we delete that 'x' → "xxy". In "magic" the first decreasing pair is m > a, delete 'm' → "agic". In "arise" the first decreasing pair is r > i, delete 'r' → "aise".

solution.approach: Scanning the string from left to right, find the first index i such that S[i] > S[i+1]. Deleting S[i] makes the prefix as small as possible while keeping the rest unchanged, which yields the overall lexicographically smallest result. If no such index exists (the string is non‑decreasing), deleting the last character is optimal because any earlier deletion would make the prefix smaller but would also shift a larger character forward. The algorithm runs in O(N) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) overall (≤ 3·10^5).
